item.title: Overwatch Just Gave Players Even More Reasons to Log In Throughout March
item.description:
Overwatch just announced that every weekend in March will be a Double Match XP weekend to celebrate the game's ongoing success. Now, Overwatch fans have even more incentive to log into the hero shooter, as if Reign of Talon Season 1 wasn't enough.

February 10 marked a new era for Future Earth. The soft relaunch of Overwatch , now without the "2" in its title, included five new heroes, a refreshed UI, and the first segment of its new year-long seasonal story. The season was a huge hit, with Overwatch breaking player records on both Steam and Twitch during its opening weeks.

Double Match XP Weekends in Overwatch All March

Overwatch is celebrating this success all March long. According to a new post on Twitter, Blizzard has begun a special “Player Appreciation Boost,” granting Double Match XP every single weekend throughout the month of March:

  • March 6–8
  • March 13–15
  • March 20–22
  • March 27–29

Double Match XP multiplies the baseline Battle Pass XP Overwatch players earn from playing games. This typically averages between 700 and 1,000 XP, with more rewarded for victories or longer games. Playing in a group adds an extra 20% boost, and queuing for in-demand roles can grant between 100 and 500 extra XP on top of that, all multiplied by the Double XP bonus. Those who capitalize on all of these bonuses together can easily get over 2,000 Battle Pass XP for a single win.

This isn’t the first Double Match XP weekend Overwatch has held to celebrate its renewed success. From February 20 through February 23, Blizzard granted this bonus along with five free Loot Boxes. There are no extra Loot Boxes to claim this time, but the Double Match XP will return more frequently to compensate.

That said, there are still a few more days left to earn some extra Loot Boxes. Twitch Drops for Overwatch Reign of Talon Season 1: Conquest are live through March 8, offering up to 10 free Loot Boxes – two of which are Legendary – simply by watching streamers on Twitch. However, it takes 13 hours to earn all of these rewards; as of press time, fewer than 55 hours remain until the campaign ends, so fans who haven't started should begin watching soon.

What’s Next in Overwatch?

Presently, Overwatch is in the midst of its fourth week of the Conquest event. This headlining gameplay activity for Reign of Talon Season 1 has players align with either Overwatch or Talon in order to complete objectives, earn rewards, and prove their loyalty. The five-week event wraps up on March 17, meaning fans will soon see which faction will reign supreme, and consequently, which Echo skin will be granted to all players.

Talon won weeks one and three, while Overwatch won week two and is poised to win week four, meaning both sides are likely to be tied going into the final week.

Overwatch Reign of Talon Season 1 is approaching its halfway point ahead of Season 2's launch. The midseason update is expected to arrive on March 10 or March 17, bringing with it a slew of new hero balance adjustments, plus a Mythic hero skin for Mei. A new collaboration between Overwatch and Nier: Automata also kicks off on March 10, featuring Adam Lifeweaver, A2 Vendetta, 9S Wuyang, 2B Kiriko, and Commander White Mercy.

Subscribe to the newsletter for essential Overwatch updates

Looking for clear breakdowns, actionable strategies, and concise previews of Overwatch events and updates? Subscribe to our newsletter for focused coverage, practical tips to maximize in-game rewards, and analysis you can use.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

The second season of Overwatch ’s Reign of Talon drops April 14, bringing with it even more surprises. A new story arc, potentially including a community event like Conquest, will arrive, along with another playable hero. Post-match accolades are coming back to the game, plus an official port for the Switch 2. Reworks to the Antarctic Peninsula map, Ramattra added to Stadium, and the Overwatch 10-year anniversary event are just some of the other features fans can expect in this upcoming update.

6 /10

Overwatch (Formerly Overwatch 2)

Follow
Followed
Shooter
FPS
MOBA
Multiplayer
Systems
OpenCritic Reviews
Top Critic Avg: 77 /100 Critics Rec: 65%
Released
August 10, 2023

8 Images

WHERE TO PLAY

Shooter, FPS, MOBA, Multiplayer
Powered by

item.title: Counting Mortal Kombat 1 and Dead by Daylight, There Have Been 21 Ghostface Killers
item.description:
Every movie in the Scream franchise ends with a big twist where the identities of the Ghostface killer or killers are revealed. The movies have featured 16 Ghostface killers by themselves, but even more were introduced in the TV series and through Ghostface's appearances in video games. Mortal Kombat 1 specifically adds two more Ghostface killers to the mix, while Dead by Daylight has its own Ghostface killer as well. Between Mortal Kombat 1 , Dead by Daylight , the movies, and the TV show, there have been 21 Ghostface killers and counting.

Scream 7 premiered in theaters on February 27 and was an instant commercial success. While Scream 7 reviews make it the lowest-rated entry in the slasher franchise to date, the film has enjoyed a great deal of success at the box office. At the time of this writing, Scream 7 has grossed an impressive $111 million at the box office against a $45 million budget.

With Scream 7 bringing the franchise back to the forefront, there has been a lot of debate about the best Ghostface killers. Most of the focus is on the Ghostface killers featured in the Scream movies, but new killers have been introduced elsewhere as well, with Mortal Kombat 1 and Dead by Daylight having their own Ghostface killers. Franchise fans that have only watched the movies may be interested in learning the identities of all the Ghostface killers the franchise has seen so far.

This article contains MAJOR SPOILERS for Scream 7, the Scream movies in general, and the Scream TV series. If you don't want to know the identity of every single Ghostface killer , do not read on.

Every Ghostface Killer in Mortal Kombat 1

  • Kira
  • Kobra

We know for certain that one of the Ghostface killers in Mortal Kombat 1 is Billy Loomis, the killer in the first movie. But there are hints in Mortal Kombat 1 that suggest that legacy characters Kira and Kobra are the other two under the Ghostface mask in the game (dialogue also hints at Scream 1 's Stu potentially being featured as well).

The Mortal Kombat 1 Ghostface killers can perform attacks associated with Kira and Kobra, who haven't been fully playable in a Mortal Kombat game since 2006's Mortal Kombat: Armageddon .

Every Ghostface Killer in Dead by Daylight

  • Danny Johnson/Jed Olsen

Dead by Daylight has its own Ghostface killer, Danny Johnson aka Jed Olsen, who is a freelance reporter that nightlights as a serial killer.

There are Ghostface killers in Call of Duty and Fortnite , but they do not appear to have specific identities like the ones in Mortal Kombat 1 and Dead by Daylight .

Every Ghostface Killer from the Scream Movies

  • Billy Loomis
  • Stu Macher
  • Nancy Loomis
  • Mickey Altieri
  • Roman Bridger
  • Jill Roberts
  • Charlie Walker
  • Richie Kirsch
  • Amber Freeman
  • Jason Carvey
  • Wayne Bailey
  • Quinn Bailey
  • Ethan Landry
  • Karl Gibbs
  • Jessica Bowden
  • Marco Davis

With the release of Scream 7 , there are now 16 Ghostface killers that come from the movies. Billy Loomis and Stu Macher are the killers in Scream 1 , while Scream 2 featured Nancy Loomis and Mickey Altieri. Scream 3 only had one Ghostface killer, Roman Bridger, while Jill Roberts and Charlie Walker were the killers in Scream 4 . Scream (2022) featured Richie Kirsch and Amber Freeman, while Scream 6 started with Jason Carvey, who was quickly killed off and replaced by Wayne Bailey, Quinn Bailey, and Ethan Landry.

Subscribe for exclusive Ghostface and Scream coverage

Joining the newsletter gives you in-depth coverage of Ghostface and Scream across film, TV, and games, with detailed analysis and cross-media breakdowns to deepen your franchise understanding and catch every twist.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

Scream 7 once again has three Ghostface killers, with Karl Gibbs killed off about halfway through the movie, and the big finale revealing Jessica Bowden and Marco Davis as the other two killers.

Every Ghostface Killer from the Scream TV Show

  • Beth
  • Jamal Elliot

The Scream TV show initially did not feature the iconic Ghostface, but the third season of the show brought the costume back. The two Ghostface killers that season were Beth and Jamal Elliot.

8 /10

Mortal Kombat 1

Follow
Followed
Fighting
Systems

item.title: 7 Best FPS Games You've Probably Never Heard Of
item.description:
First-person shooters are inescapable, but quite a few games slip through the cracks and gain little attention outside their small audience. While Call of Duty , Battlefield , and Halo will always and forever be big deals, there are plenty of under-the-radar FPS games that feature fantastic gunplay, innovative mechanics, and addictive gameplay.

Players who are craving unique settings, quirky stories, unusual weapons, or even just a solid shooter will need to look beyond the mainstream or revisit cult classics from the past. To make that process easier, let's highlight a few great FPS games you might not have heard of .

While most of the games are overall unknown, we will include two releases that were pretty popular during their initial runs but have faded from the spotlight. These two games are unlikely to ever get sequels, so they deserve a recommendation so that they are not forgotten by newer generations.

7 Brigand: Oaxaca

An Indie Immersive Sim That Lets You Role-Play In Post-Apocalyptic Mexico

Brigand: Oaxaca
FPS
RPG
Immersive Sim
Systems
Released
July 12, 2017

WHERE TO PLAY

DIGITAL

Developer(s)
Brian J. Lancaster, Laughing Coyote Software
Genre(s)
FPS, RPG, Immersive Sim
Platform(s)
PC
Powered by

We don't get all that many RPG FPS hybrids, particularly ones that take inspiration from immersive sims like Deus Ex , S.T.A.L.K.E.R. , and Fallout: New Vegas . While its old-school visuals might take some getting used to, Brigand: Oaxaca is an ambitious indie game that sought to deliver a proper role-playing experience set in post-apocalyptic southern Mexico. Following a catastrophe, humanity has dwindled down to its base components, meaning faction warfare, scavengers, and cults.

As long as you don't mind mediocre and sometimes janky gunplay, Brigand: Oaxaca should absolutely hit the mark for any fans craving an RPG-first shooter set in a surprisingly large world for this type of release. With survival mechanics, chaotic situations with multiple solutions, systemic gameplay, and a darkly comedic tone, Brigand accomplishes a lot considering it was conceived by one creator (with some assistance).

6 Bulletstorm

When Trash Talk is More than Encouraged

Bulletstorm
7 /10
Released
February 22, 2011

WHERE TO PLAY

DIGITAL

ESRB
M For Mature 17+ due to Blood and Gore, Game Experience May Change During Online Play, Intense Violence, Partial Nudity, Sexual Themes, Strong Language, Use of Alcohol
Developer(s)
People Can Fly
Genre(s)
First-Person Shooter
Platform(s)
PS3, PS4, Xbox One, Xbox 360, PC
Powered by

Some FPS games take themselves way too seriously. Then there’s Bulletstorm , which gleefully throws subtlety out the window and opts for pure, chaotic fun. Set in a world where profanity is practically a second language, players take on the role of Grayson Hunt, a washed-up space pirate seeking revenge on a corrupt general. The story is hilariously over-the-top, but the real draw is the Skillshot system.

Players earn points by creatively dispatching their enemies, like kicking them into electrified barriers or shooting them in particularly unpleasant areas. It’s all about improvisation and racking up combos, turning firefights into chaotic spectacles of carnage. This game never gained mainstream success, partly because it leaned too hard into its crude humor, but those who stuck around found a surprisingly well-crafted shooter underneath the bravado.

If you grew up with the PS3 era, you probably heard of Bulletstorm ; however, younger players are likely unfamiliar with it. Honestly, Bulletstorm is one of my favorite FPS games, so I just wanted to bring it up.

5 Darkest Of Days

The FPS That Sends You To The Wrong Place At The Wrong Time

Darkest of Days
First-Person Shooter
Systems
Released
September 7, 2009

WHERE TO PLAY

PHYSICAL

ESRB
M // Blood, Strong Language, Violence
Developer(s)
8monkey Labs
Genre(s)
First-Person Shooter
Platform(s)
PC, Xbox 360
8 Images
Powered by

Darkest of Days is what happens when a game’s concept is way cooler than its execution, but it’s still a wild ride for those who stick with it. The premise is simple: a soldier from Custer’s Last Stand gets recruited by a time-traveling organization to correct historical events. This means players find themselves fighting in ancient Rome one minute and in World War 1 trenches the next.

The game’s biggest selling point is the weapon variety. In one level, players are stuck with a single-shot musket, and in another, they’re mowing down Roman soldiers with futuristic firearms. It’s ridiculous in the best way. Unfortunately, the graphics are a bit dated, and the AI isn’t the sharpest, but the sheer novelty of fighting through famous battles with wildly inappropriate weaponry makes it memorable.

4 The Darkness 2

When Darkness Takes Over... Twice

The Darkness 2
Released
February 7, 2012

WHERE TO PLAY

DIGITAL
PHYSICAL

ESRB
m
Developer(s)
Digital Extremes
Genre(s)
First-Person Shooter
Platform(s)
PS3, Xbox 360, PC
Powered by

Unlike most shooters that stick to realism or sci-fi, The Darkness 2 goes full comic book mode, leaning into its graphic novel roots with brutal, cel-shaded visuals and a story that’s equal parts gritty and tragic. Players control Jackie Estacado, a mob boss cursed with a demonic power known as The Darkness. It’s a tale of revenge, betrayal, and inner demons—literally.

What makes The Darkness 2 stand out is the way it lets players dual-wield both firearms and demon arms, creating some truly chaotic combat scenarios. There’s something unnervingly satisfying about using demonic tentacles to skewer an enemy while unloading a shotgun in the other direction. It’s stylish, violent, and surprisingly heartfelt, with Jackie’s struggle to control his dark side adding depth to the carnage.

Similar to Bulletstorm , PS3-era players probably have fond memories of both The Darkness games. Since then, the series has slipped from the public consciousness.

3 Shattered Horizon

Zero Gravity Makes Zero Sense (But It's Still Cool)

Shattered Horizon
FPS
Tactical
Systems
Released
November 4, 2009
Developer(s)
Futuremark
Publisher(s)
Futuremark

Genre(s)
FPS, Tactical
Platform(s)
PC
Powered by

In a world where most FPS games keep players glued to the ground, Shattered Horizon literally launches them into space. Developed by Futuremark, this multiplayer shooter takes place in the debris field of a massive space station explosion. Players must navigate zero-gravity combat, which means thinking in all three dimensions rather than just running and gunning.

The novelty of floating and shooting in every direction makes for a steep learning curve, but the sense of freedom is unmatched. There’s nothing quite like sneaking up on someone from below while they’re busy defending a higher platform. Sadly, the lack of a conventional single-player campaign and dwindling player base kept it from thriving, but those who experienced its unique take on FPS mechanics still remember it fondly. AI bots were eventually added, so people who own the game can still play to an extend.

2 Receiver

Guns Aren't Just for Shooting

Receiver
FPS
Roguelike
Action
Systems
Released
April 29, 2013

WHERE TO PLAY

DIGITAL

Developer(s)
Wolfire Games
Genre(s)
FPS, Roguelike, Action
Platform(s)
PC
Powered by

Receiver is the polar opposite of the typical FPS. Instead of bombastic firefights, it focuses on meticulous gun handling and realistic mechanics. Every bullet must be loaded manually, every safety toggled, and every hammer cocked. It’s more of a gun simulator than a traditional shooter, but that’s where its charm lies.

Subscribe to the newsletter for hidden FPS game finds

Unearth more underrated shooters - subscribing to the newsletter delivers curated deep dives on under-the-radar FPS gems, cult classics, and smart recommendations, plus other gaming discoveries, so you can find standout guns, settings, and mechanics.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

Set in a dystopian world where rogue drones patrol empty buildings, players must collect audio tapes to piece together a fragmented narrative. One wrong move means death, and the procedural level design keeps every run fresh. There’s an almost meditative quality to the slow, cautious gameplay. It’s not everyone’s cup of tea, but those who appreciate realism and strategy will find it strangely captivating.

1 E.Y.E.: Divine Cybermancy

Cyberpunk Gets Weird

E.Y.E.: Divine Cybermancy
FPS
Action RPG
Systems
Released
July 29, 2011

WHERE TO PLAY

DIGITAL

ESRB
m
Genre(s)
FPS, Action RPG
Powered by

E.Y.E.: Divine Cybermancy is easily the most ambitious game on this list, but it’s also the most confusing. Created by a small indie studio, it mashes together cyberpunk themes, RPG mechanics, and an incomprehensible plot about a secretive organization fighting rival factions. Players are cyber-enhanced warriors with access to psychic abilities, hacking, and a ridiculous arsenal of firearms.

The game’s biggest appeal is its sheer unpredictability. One minute, players are sneaking through a futuristic city, and the next, they’re facing supernatural horrors. The janky controls and steep learning curve don’t help, but its cult following swears by E.Y.E.'s bizarre charm. It’s a fever dream of an FPS, and honestly, that’s what makes it so hard to forget.

item.title: Runner Shell Tier List in Marathon
item.description:
There are 7 Runner Shells (including Rook) for players to try out in Marathon . However, the complexity of their abilities and their synergy with each other might confuse some players who are looking to raid alone or with others.

Below, we've compiled all Runners in Marathon and created a Tier List that ranks them from best to worst, including their strengths and weaknesses. You'll find a Tier List along with why we've ranked them where they are, so that you can stay on top of the UESC and other Runners.

Runner Tier List in Marathon

Best & Worst Runner Shells

Tier

Runner Shell

Why It's Ranked Here

S-Tier

Triage

Great for Squads, as Triage's Med Drone allows you and your team to remain healed in and out of fights. The Reboot+ Ultimate is also great for instantly reviving allies near and far.

S-Tier

Assassin

For solo players or those in a squad who love to flank, Assassin is great. His Active Camo enables him to enter invisibility, and Smoke Screen will help him disrupt enemy optics as he fights from the shadows.

S-Tier

Vandal

An anarchist in combat who specializes in movement and maneuverability. Vandal is designed to be fast thanks to a double jump and faster sprint, aided by the Amplify Ultimate. She's a character to use if you like staying agile.

A-Tier

Recon

Recon helps paranoid players map out areas by tracking hostile enemies, whether that's UESC or other Runners. While useful, the limited range and timer on their abilities stop them from being S-Tier.

A-Tier

Destroyer

The Riot Barricade is pretty decent for distracting enemies while your squad deals damage. The Search and Destroy Ultimate really helps lay down suppressing fire thanks to its missiles, and the boost to sprint speeds helps the Destroyer get into combat fast.

B-Tier

Thief

This Runner is designed for hoarders and those who want to stay out of the fight. The Grapple Device enables Thief to escape or reach high places, and the Pickpocket Drone can sweep up small loot. It's not a viable kit for combat.

B-Tier

Rook

A solo-exclusive Runner that can blend in with UESC. Fairly decent for solo runs, but limited due to a forced Sponsored Kit and the inability to complete Contracts.

S-Tier Marathon Runners

Triage

Triage is a must-have for squads. Triage is a field medic, and with the Med-Drone Ability, they can deploy healing drones on themselves and their allies that work for both health and shields.

Triage's Reboot+ Prime Ability also helps in tough-to-win fights, as they can instantly revive teammates and even bring back their corpses to ensure they're back in the fight without delay.

Assassin

Assassin is all about stealth, and that's beneficial for solo and squads. For solo players, Assassin can be used to get the drop on enemies or to avoid them completely as you loot up thanks to the Active Camo Tactical Ability. Failing at stealth, the Smoke Screen Prime Ability always aids in a fast escape.

Assassin's benefits for squads really come into play when encountering a hostile team of Runners, as Assassin can usually move behind valuable enemies and flank them for easy kills while they're focused on your squadmates.

Vandal

Vandal is great for PvP-focused players. This troublemaker has amplified movement to ensure she's covering the high ground at greater speeds and heights than other Runners. She's great for those who like to fight with an advantage.

Vandal's faster movement is great for getting around the map and losing enemies, and her defensive capabilities with the Disrupt Cannon ensure she has a trick up her sleeve for when she needs to fight.

A-Tier Marathon Runners

Destroyer

Destroyer is an underrated combat specialist that players can use for both solos and squads. With Destroyer, it's all about being the team's tank. The Riot Barricade provides a generous shield that replenishes charge, and it can be used to distract enemies as a bullet sponge.

When under heavy fire, Search and Destroy becomes an essential Prime Ability, as dealing damage will enable automatically-locked missiles to shoot for extra damage. Destroyer's Thruster and Tactical Sprint also give him deeper mobility in combat to rush to battle or run from it.

Recon

Recon's main focus is on intel, allowing a squad or a solo player to see threats before they see you. Recon's Echo Pulse and Tracker Drone Abilities enable Recon to locate and identify threats from UESC to hostile Runners, even with a varied cooldown.

Recon can really help paranoid players to identify the threats that are within a POI before taking a dangerous journey inside, as knowing where enemies are will be far more advantageous than listening for footsteps, or worse, gunfire that's already aimed at your head.

B-Tier Marathon Runners

Thief

Thief is all about looting and staying away from enemies. While a fairly decent choice for solo players, Thief struggles to bring any help to her team. The Pickpocket Drone is mainly just to suck up loot while hiding from hostiles, and the Grapple Device is used to get away more than it's used to get into fights.

Rook

Rook really deserves its own ranking, as this scavenger is designed for solo-only plays. With a limited loadout and an inability to complete contracts, Rook's whole gimmick is to get into a raid, scavenge what's been left behind by other Runners, and get out.

Subscribe to the Newsletter for Marathon Runner Builds

Sharpen your Marathon play—subscribe to the newsletter for deeper runner coverage: detailed tier breakdowns, role-specific strategies, build suggestions, and matchup advice for Triage, Assassin, Vandal and more Marathon runner-focused insights to refine solo or squad tactics.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

Rook's abilities are all about camouflage and independence, and additional upgrades from Factions will make Rook a competent Runner for those who want to continue on this scavenger run.

Marathon

Follow
Followed
Extraction Shooter
FPS
Multiplayer
Systems
Released
March 5, 2026
ESRB
Teen / Animated Blood, Language, Violence, In-Game Purchases, Users Interact
Developer(s)
Bungie
Publisher(s)
Bungie
Multiplayer
Online Multiplayer, Online Co-Op
6 Images

WHERE TO PLAY

DIGITAL

Genre(s)
Extraction Shooter, FPS, Multiplayer
Powered by

item.title: You Might Be Able to Play Marvel's Spider-Man or God of War Ragnarok on the Next-Gen Xbox
item.description:
Xbox ’s console endeavors have been a mixed bag, to say the least. The company built a strong reputation with the original Xbox, solidified its dominance with the Xbox 360, began to lose its grip on the market with the Xbox One, and seems to have fully thrown in the towel during the Xbox Series X/S generation, at least when it comes to longstanding industry practices like exclusive games. But Xbox is not abandoning its hardware ambitions altogether.

The next Xbox console was just announced by the official Xbox Twitter account, which refers to it as Project Helix. In conjunction with this announcement was a tweet from newly anointed Xbox Gaming CEO Asha Sharma, wherein she declared that “Project Helix will lead in performance and play your Xbox and PC games.” The “lead in performance” bit is to be expected—basically every new console, outside those specifically marketed as lower-spec budget options, is deemed the “most powerful” by its creators. But the comment about playing both Xbox and PC games is interesting, if only for the fact that it essentially confirms something that has been widely reported and speculated about for months: the next Xbox will be, more or less, a console-PC hybrid, which could lead to some very strange outcomes indeed.

The Next ‘Xbox’ Might be Able to Play PlayStation Exclusives

Over the past few years, Sony has brought several of its most high-profile exclusives to PC, including:

  • Ghost of Tsushima
  • Marvel’s Spider-Man
  • Marvel’s Spider-Man: Miles Morales
  • Marvel’s Spider-Man 2
  • God of War
  • God of War Ragnarok
  • Uncharted 4
  • The Last of Us Part 1
  • The Last of Us Part 2

If you ask a PlayStation user what some of the best PS exclusives are, there’s a good chance they’ll name at least one of the aforementioned. Their migration to PC is therefore quite significant, as these are basically what sell PlayStation consoles. The working assumption has long been that Sony hasn’t viewed PC as a competing platform in the same way it views the Switch or Xbox. PC users are probably the least likely to abandon their hardware in favor of a home console, and they represent game sales that Sony is missing out on.

This makes a lot of sense, and the data seems to back it up: 2018’s God of War reportedly sold over 4-million copies on Steam since its 2022 port—not bad for a four-year-old game. But matters are getting considerably more complicated, especially in the wake of Project Helix’s announcement. If the next Xbox can play PC games, reportedly from Steam (after all, that’s what the ROG Xbox Ally can do), then that means that every PlayStation exclusive that’s received a PC version will technically be playable on an Xbox device. The idea of playing The Last of Us or God of War on a Microsoft home console is a little hard to get used to, but it would be a worthwhile gain for Xbox, which has been porting several of its most high-profile games to PlayStation for the past few years, giving its own audience nothing in return.

Don’t Get Used to the Idea of Playing PlayStation Games On the New Xbox

As it happens, a recent report from Jason Schreier, a longtime industry insider for Bloomberg, alleges that Sony is going to be massively scaling back its PC releases. According to Bloomberg, there are no plans to bring Ghost of Yotei or Saros , one of 2026’s most anticipated PS5 exclusives, to PC. Project Helix might be able to run Ghost of Tsushima , but it probably won’t run Ghost of Yotei at any point. You may be able to play Returnal on the device, but not Saros . Schrier’s report says that Sony’s multiplayer games are still expected to launch on PC, however.

Subscribe for expert newsletter coverage of Project Helix

Want deeper context on Project Helix and what console-PC hybrids mean for exclusives? Subscribe to the newsletter for focused analysis, expert takes, and clear breakdowns of the industry shifts shaping gaming platforms.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

Schreier’s report says that PC sales of the aforementioned games have not met Sony’s expectations, although the imminent (if RAM shortages ease up) release of the Steam Machine may have played a role in this decision as well. It’s also likely that Sony saw the writing on the wall with respect to Xbox, sensing, as many consumers have, its pivot to a PC-console hybrid approach. If audiences can play PlayStation games on a Valve or Microsoft device, it could undermine the PlayStation brand. This is doubly true if Valve or Microsoft can really compete with Sony on the power and pricing fronts.

Still, I don’t see a reality where Sony actually pulls its existing PC ports from online stores or anything like that. The bell has been rung, as it were, and doing something so radical would probably only serve to generate negative publicity with no real financial or strategic benefit. So, Sony seems to be doubling down on its classic exclusive practices, but several of its tentpole releases of the past decade will probably still be available on PC, and thereby, the next Xbox. This is certainly not what I would have expected the “console wars” to look like for the next generation, but it should be interesting to watch unfold.
